verses.text: When thou goest out to battle and seest horses, chariots, a people more than thou — be not afraid: the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee out of Egypt. The priest shall speak: Hear, Israel, let not your hearts faint, fear not. The LORD your God goeth with you, to fight for you, to save you. Guidance: The first soldier on the field is the priest. Before swords are drawn, words are spoken — fear-not, He fights for you. Israel's warfare started with sermons, not strategy. The army that hears Egypt remembered does not panic at chariots.

The officers shall speak: What man hath built a new house, not dedicated it? let him return. What man hath planted a vineyard, not yet eaten? let him return. What man hath betrothed a wife, not taken her? let him return. What man is fearful and fainthearted? let him return, lest his brethren's heart faint as well. Guidance: Four exemptions — a new house, a young vineyard, a fresh marriage, a frightened heart. God's wars do not consume the half-built life. And the fearful man is sent home for the sake of those around him — fear is contagious. The kingdom releases the unsteady before they ruin steady ones.

When thou comest nigh unto a city to fight, proclaim peace unto it. If it answer peace, the people shall serve thee. If it make no peace but war, besiege it. When the LORD hath delivered it, smite every male with the sword. The women, little ones, cattle, spoil shalt thou take. So shalt thou do unto all cities far off, not of these nations. Guidance: Peace was always the first offer. Only if a distant city chose war did war follow — and even then, women and children were spared from the sword. The law's restraint is striking by ancient standards. A real attempt at peace must precede every siege.

Of the cities of these people which the LORD doth give thee for an inheritance, thou shalt save alive nothing that breatheth — utterly destroy the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites. That they teach you not to do after their abominations, which they have done unto their gods. Guidance: The seven nations got no offer of peace — a unique covenant exception. The reason given: their religion would teach Israel to burn children. This was a herem judgment on cultic systems, not a pattern for any other war. Geographically bounded, theologically specific, never to be generalized.

When thou shalt besiege a city long, thou shalt not destroy the trees thereof by forcing an axe against them — for thou mayest eat of them; thou shalt not cut them down (the tree of the field is man's life): only trees thou knowest are not for meat, mayest destroy, build bulwarks against the city. Guidance: Even in war, the orchards are protected. Fruit trees take years to bear; cutting them is theft from the future. God writes ecological mercy into the siege manual. The tree is man's life — the next generation's food. Wars end; trees should still feed somebody.
